[5252]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 自云 - Classical expression meaning 'claimed to be' or 'said he was.'
  • 好音乐医术之事 - Means 'fond of music and medical skills,' indicating a broad interest in these fields.
  • 体有臊气 - Literally 'body has a foul smell,' suggesting an unusual or unpleasant odor.
  • 恒以名香自防 - Means 'always used fragrant incense to protect himself,' indicating a habitual practice.
  • 唯忌猛犬 - Means 'only feared fierce dogs,' suggesting a specific phobia.
  • 自审死日 - Means 'examined his own death day,' indicating a premonition of death.
  • 气絶便殡 - Means 'once the breath leaves, immediately bury,' indicating a strict command regarding the burial.
  • 勿令狗见我尸也 - Means 'do not let dogs see my body,' indicating a specific concern.
  • 死于山阳 - Means 'died in Shanyang,' specifying the location of death.
  • 敛毕觉棺空 - Means 'after the coffin was sealed, it was found to be empty,' indicating a mysterious event.
  • 看不见尸体 - Means 'the body could not be found,' indicating the disappearance of the body.
  • 时人咸谓狐也 - Means 'people at the time all said it was a fox,' indicating a common belief.
  • 北齐后主武平中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Zhan of Northern Qi during the Wu Ping period.
  • 朔州府门无故有小儿脚迹 - Means 'there were suddenly footprints of a child at the gate of Shuozhou Prefecture,' indicating an unexplained phenomenon.
  • 拥土为城雉之状 - Means 'piled earth to form the shape of a city wall,' indicating a strange behavior.
  • 察之乃狐媚 - Means 'upon investigation, it was found to be a fox's trick,' indicating a supernatural explanation.
  • 隋南阳西郊有一亭 - Means 'in the western suburbs of Nanyang during the Sui dynasty, there was a pavilion,' specifying the setting.
  • 人不可止 - Means 'people could not stay,' indicating an inexplicable occurrence.
  • 止则有祸 - Means 'if they stayed, they would suffer misfortune,' indicating a warning.
  • 邑人宋大贤以正道自处 - Means 'the townsman Song Daxian maintained himself with righteous conduct,' indicating a virtuous character.
  • 尝宿亭楼夜坐鼓琴 - Means 'once stayed in the pavilion, sat at night, and played the zither,' indicating a peaceful activity.
  • 与大贤语矃目磋齿 - Means 'spoke to Song Daxian, squinting and showing teeth,' indicating a threatening appearance.
  • 大贤鼓琴如故 - Means 'Song Daxian continued playing the zither as before,' indicating composure.
  • 于市中取死人头来还 - Means 'took a dead person's head from the market and returned,' indicating a macabre action.
  • 宁可少睡耶 - Means 'can you sleep a little?' indicating a question.
  • 因以死人头投大贤前 - Means 'then threw the dead person's head in front of Song Daxian,' indicating an act of aggression.
  • 甚佳吾暮卧无枕正欲得此 - Means 'it's very good, I don't have a pillow for my evening rest, just what I wanted,' indicating a sarcastic response.
  • 良久乃还 - Means 'after a long time, it returned,' indicating a delayed return.
  • 宁可共手搏耶 - Means 'can we fight hand-to-hand?' indicating a challenge.
  • 大贤曰善 - Means 'Song Daxian said, 'Good,' indicating agreement.
  • 语未竟在前大贤便逆捉其腰 - Means 'before the words were finished, Song Daxian grabbed its waist,' indicating quick action.
  • 大贤遂杀之 - Means 'Song Daxian then killed it,' indicating the outcome.
  • 明日视之乃是老狐也 - Means 'the next day, it was found to be an old fox,' indicating the true identity.
  • 自此亭舍更无妖怪 - Means 'from then on, the pavilion and its surroundings had no more supernatural occurrences,' indicating a resolution.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 胡道洽 - A historical figure from the Tang dynasty known for his musical and medical skills.
  • 广陵 - An ancient name for Yangzhou, a city in Jiangsu province, known for its cultural and historical significance.
  • 北齐后主 - Refers to Emperor Zhan of Northern Qi, who ruled during the Northern and Southern Dynasties period.
  • 武平 - The reign title of Emperor Zhan.
  • 朔州 - A prefecture in Shanxi province, known for its strategic importance.
  • 安南正 - A historical figure who rebelled in Beishuo during the Northern Qi period.
  • 隋南阳 - Refers to Nanyang during the Sui dynasty, a region in Henan province.
  • 宋大贤 - A virtuous townsman from the Sui dynasty, known for his righteous conduct.

Proper Nouns

  • 胡道洽 - A historical figure from the Tang dynasty.
  • 广陵 - An ancient name for Yangzhou.
  • 北齐后主 - Emperor Zhan of Northern Qi.
  • 武平 - The reign title of Emperor Zhan.
  • 朔州 - A prefecture in Shanxi province.
  • 安南正 - A historical figure who rebelled in Beishuo.
  • 隋南阳 - Nanyang during the Sui dynasty.
  • 宋大贤 - A virtuous townsman from the Sui dynasty.

[5253]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 殊宠 - Means 'exceptional favor' or 'special grace,' indicating the high status of Longsun Wuji.
  • 狐媚 - Means 'fox charm' or 'fox enchantment,' referring to a supernatural or mystical influence.
  • 诏诸术士 - Means 'the emperor summoned various exorcists,' indicating the involvement of multiple experts in dealing with the supernatural.
  • 诏书见召 - Means 'an imperial summons is expected,' indicating the anticipation of official orders.
  • 天狐 - Refers to a celestial fox, a supernatural being with divine powers.
  • 受赂 - Means 'accepting bribes,' suggesting that the celestial fox was not acting out of malice but perhaps due to external influence.
  • 设案几坐书一符 - Means 'he placed a table and wrote a talisman,' indicating the use of a magical symbol to counteract the supernatural influence.
  • 敕诣无忌家 - Means 'the emperor ordered him to visit Longsun Wuji's home,' indicating the official nature of the visit.
  • 敕诣 - Means 'the emperor ordered him to visit,' reinforcing the official nature of the visit.
  • 敕 - Refers to the emperor's decree or order, emphasizing the authority behind the action.

[5254]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 刀箭云适已苦战被伤 - Descriptive phrase indicating that the individual has endured intense combat and been injured.
  • 言毕散去 - Means 'having spoken, they departed,' indicating a conclusion to a conversation or statement.
  • 天地忽尔昏暝 - Describes a sudden darkness over the sky, often used to indicate supernatural events.
  • 下阶小屈膝 - A gesture of respect or submission, involving a slight bow.
  • 诸神立视而已 - Means 'the gods stood and watched,' indicating a passive observation by divine beings.
  • 五岳神也 - Refers to the five mountain deities, a common concept in Chinese mythology.
  • 无狐魅不成村 - A proverb meaning 'without fox spirits, there would be no village,' suggesting the presence of supernatural beings in rural areas.
  • 肆行奸私 - Means 'to act on one's own desires without restraint,' often used to describe immoral behavior.
  • 神道所殛 - Means 'punished by the divine way,' indicating divine retribution.
  • 量决五下 - Means 'to be punished with five strokes,' a form of corporal punishment.
  • 血流满地 - Describes a scene where blood covers the ground, indicating severe punishment.
  • 不以为快 - Means 'not finding it satisfying,' indicating a lack of satisfaction or relief.
  • 恨杖少 - Means 'regretting the lack of punishment,' indicating a desire for more severe punishment.
  • 五下是人间五百 - Means 'five strokes are equivalent to five hundred in the human world,' suggesting a disproportionate punishment.
  • 为天曹役使 - Means 'to serve as an agent of heaven,' indicating a supernatural role.
  • 敕自尔不得复至相公家 - Means 'ordered not to return to the lord's house,' indicating a divine command.
  • 疾遂愈 - Means 'the illness was cured,' indicating a positive outcome.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 五岳神 - Refers to the five mountain deities in Chinese mythology, each associated with a specific mountain and direction.
  • 无狐魅不成村 - A proverb that reflects the belief in the prevalence of fox spirits in rural areas, often associated with folklore and superstition.
  • 五岳 - The five sacred mountains in Chinese geography: Mount Tai, Mount Hua, Mount Heng (Hunan), Mount Heng (Shanxi), and Mount Song.
  • 五下是人间五百 - A reference to the severe corporal punishment in ancient China, where five strokes could be equivalent to a much harsher punishment in the human world.
  • 天曹 - Refers to the heavenly bureaucracy, indicating a divine or supernatural authority.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 刀箭云适已苦战被伤终不可得言毕散去 - This long sentence describes the individual's injuries and departure, with the phrase '终不可得' indicating a finality.
  • 天地忽尔昏暝帝及无忌惧而入室俄闻虚空有兵马声须臾见五人各长数丈来诣崔所行列致敬崔乃下阶小屈膝寻呼帝及无忌出拜庭中诸神立视而已 - This complex sentence describes a supernatural event, with multiple clauses and actions.
  • 崔云相公家有媚狐敢烦执事取之诸神敬诺遂各散去 - This sentence describes the interaction between the individual and the gods, with the phrase '敢烦执事' indicating a polite request.
  • 无忌不胜愤恚遂以长剑所之狐初不惊崔云此已通神击之无益自取困耳 - This sentence describes the individual's reaction to the fox, with the phrase '自取困耳' indicating that the individual's actions are futile.
  • 崔取东引桃枝决之血流满地无忌不以为快但恨杖少崔云五下是人间五百殊非小刑为天曹役使此辈杀之不可使敕自尔不得复至相公家狐乃飞去 - This sentence describes the punishment of the fox and the individual's reaction, with the phrase '殊非小刑' indicating the severity of the punishment.
  • 疾遂愈 - A simple statement indicating the resolution of the illness, with the phrase '疾遂愈' indicating a positive outcome.

Proper Nouns

  • 崔 - The name of the individual who interacts with the gods and the fox.
  • 帝 - Refers to the emperor or a high-ranking official.
  • 无忌 - The name of the individual who is associated with the emperor and is involved in the supernatural events.
  • 五岳神 - The five mountain deities, each associated with a specific mountain and direction.
  • 天曹 - The heavenly bureaucracy, indicating a divine or supernatural authority.

[5255]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 野狐假简形 - Idiom meaning 'a fox impersonating Zhang Jian'; used to describe someone pretending to be another person.
  • 讲罢归舍 - Means 'after finishing the lecture and returning home'.
  • 络丝 - Refers to the act of spinning thread.
  • 元不见兄来 - Means 'I didn't see my brother coming'.
  • 化为野狐而走 - Means 'transformed into a fox and fled'.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐 - Refers to the Tang dynasty (618-907 AD).
  • 国子监 - Refers to the Imperial Academy, the highest educational institution in China during the Tang dynasty.
  • 张简 - A historical figure, a teaching assistant at the Imperial Academy.
  • 河南缑氏 - Refers to a place in Henan province.
  • 弥勒佛 - Refers to Maitreya Buddha, the future Buddha in Buddhist tradition.
  • 永徽 - Refers to the Yonghui era (650-655 AD) of the Tang dynasty.
  • 太原 - Refers to Taiyuan, a city in Shanxi province.
  • 释迦 - Refers to Siddhartha Gautama, the historical Buddha.
  • 阎浮提 - Refers to Jambudvipa, the southern continent in Buddhist cosmology.

Proper Nouns

  • 张简 - A historical figure, a teaching assistant at the Imperial Academy.
  • 河南缑氏 - Refers to a place in Henan province.
  • 弥勒佛 - Refers to Maitreya Buddha, the future Buddha in Buddhist tradition.
  • 太原 - Refers to Taiyuan, a city in Shanxi province.
  • 释迦 - Refers to Siddhartha Gautama, the historical Buddha.
  • 阎浮提 - Refers to Jambudvipa, the southern continent in Buddhist cosmology.

[5256]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 麟德时 - Refers to the *Linde* era, a period during the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Tang (660-664 AD).
  • 绛州司马 - A position in the Tang dynasty administrative system, referring to the governor of Jiàngzhou.
  • 郎州佐之子 - Refers to a son of a local official, indicating social status.
  • 形迹 - Here it means 'behavior' or 'conduct.'
  • 方便 - A Buddhist term meaning 'skillful means' or 'appropriate method.'
  • 徙倚 - Means 'to linger' or 'to徘徊' (wander around).
  • 宛转而朴擒获之 - Describes the process of capturing the fox, using a method that involves making it turn and then seizing it.
  • 腔幢 - Refers to the body of the fox, possibly with a hint of ritual or ceremonial significance.
  • 衣缞绖 - Refers to wearing mourning clothes, indicating grief or sorrow.
  • 腔幢数十日 - Refers to the fox's body being observed for several days after its capture.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 麟德 - A specific reign era of the Tang dynasty, indicating the historical context of the story.
  • 上官翼 - The name of the protagonist, a historical figure from the Tang dynasty.
  • 绛州 - A location in the Tang dynasty, now part of modern-day Shanxi province.
  • 郎州佐 - Refers to a local official, indicating the social hierarchy of the time.
  • 魅 - Refers to a female ghost or spirit, a common theme in Chinese folklore.
  • 老狐 - Refers to an old fox, often associated with cunning and trickery in Chinese mythology.
  • 烧毁 - Refers to burning, a common method of dealing with supernatural threats in Chinese folklore.
  • 合家欢庆 - Refers to the entire family celebrating, indicating a positive outcome after the resolution of the conflict.

Proper Nouns

  • 上官翼 - The name of the protagonist, a historical figure from the Tang dynasty.
  • 绛州 - A location in the Tang dynasty, now part of modern-day Shanxi province.
  • 郎州佐 - Refers to a local official, indicating the social hierarchy of the time.
  • 魅 - Refers to a female ghost or spirit, a common theme in Chinese folklore.
  • 老狐 - Refers to an old fox, often associated with cunning and trickery in Chinese mythology.
  • 烧毁 - Refers to burning, a common method of dealing with supernatural threats in Chinese folklore.
  • 合家欢庆 - Refers to the entire family celebrating, indicating a positive outcome after the resolution of the conflict.

[5257]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 心所在女必知之 - A phrase indicating that the bodhisattva has the ability to know the state of a person's mind.
  • 词屈 - Means 'words fail' or 'unable to respond.'
  • 眞菩萨 - Refers to a genuine bodhisattva, as opposed to an imposter.
  • 风神邈然 - Describes a dignified and distant demeanor.
  • 四果阿罗汉地 - Refers to the fourth stage of enlightenment in Buddhism, known as Arhatship.
  • 下阶而走 - Describes the act of descending the steps and fleeing.
  • 不知所适 - Means 'do not know where to go' or 'at a loss.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐则天 - Refers to Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history, who reigned during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 垂拱初 - Refers to the early years of Wu Zetian's reign, specifically the period when she was empress regnant.
  • 谯国公李崇义 - Refers to Li Chongyi, a high-ranking official during Wu Zetian's reign.
  • 垂拱 - The era name of Wu Zetian's reign.
  • 广异记 - A collection of strange and supernatural stories from the Tang Dynasty.
  • 太平广记 - A vast compendium of Chinese stories and legends compiled during the Song Dynasty.

Proper Nouns

  • 大安和尚 - The name of the Buddhist monk.
  • 唐则天 - The name of Wu Zetian.
  • 谯国公李崇义 - The name of Li Chongyi.
  • 垂拱 - The era name of Wu Zetian's reign.
  • 广异记 - The title of the source text.
  • 太平广记 - The title of the compendium.
  • 四库全书 - The title of the imperial library collection.

[5258]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 黜 - Means 'to demote' or 'to dismiss from office', indicating a formal removal from a position.
  • 講授為業 - Means 'to teach as a profession', highlighting the speaker's occupation.
  • 術敎 - Refers to 'magical teaching' or 'esoteric instruction', suggesting a form of spiritual or mystical practice.
  • 野狐 - Literally 'wild fox', but in this context, it likely refers to a spirit or demon in the form of a fox.
  • 使野狐 - Means 'to command or summon the wild fox', indicating a magical or supernatural act.
  • 不伏 - Means 'does not submit' or 'does not obey', describing the fox's resistance.
  • 羣狐競來 - Means 'a group of foxes came rushing', indicating a chaotic and overwhelming situation.
  • 瓦甓 - Refers to 'tiles and bricks', used metaphorically for projectiles.
  • 裂碎其書 - Means 'to tear and break his books', indicating damage to the speaker's written work.
  • 空中有聲 - Means 'a voice from the air', suggesting a supernatural or divine communication.
  • 有何神術 - Means 'what kind of divine skill', indicating a question about the speaker's abilities.
  • 使我乎 - Means 'to make me do something', suggesting a command or request.
  • 竟不能禁止 - Means 'could not ultimately stop', indicating the speaker's inability to control the situation.
  • 無何而卒 - Means 'not long after, he died', indicating a sudden and unexpected death.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐 - Refers to the Tang Dynasty (618-907 AD), a period of significant cultural and literary development in China.
  • 御史 - Refers to a high-ranking official responsible for oversight and investigation, indicating the speaker's former position.
  • 萊州司戸叅軍 - Refers to a specific administrative position in the state of Lai, suggesting the speaker's previous role in local government.
  • 魏州 - Refers to the state of Wei, indicating the speaker's current place of residence and teaching.
  • 郭無為 - Refers to a person who practiced esoteric or magical teachings, suggesting a figure with supernatural abilities.
  • 老君廟 - Refers to a temple dedicated to Laozi, the founder of Taoism, indicating a place of religious significance.
  • 上巳日 - Refers to the third day of the third lunar month, a traditional Chinese festival associated with purification and spring.
  • 洛中 - Refers to the area around the city of Luoyang, indicating a specific geographical location.
  • 恭文 - Refers to a poem by Zhang Shengyang (張孟陽), indicating a literary reference.
  • 原陵 - Refers to the tomb of Emperor Guangwu of Han, indicating a historical and cultural landmark.
  • 北邙 - Refers to the northern hills near Luoyang, known for their cemeteries and tombs.
  • 徽安門 - Refers to a gate in the city of Luoyang, indicating a specific location.
  • 榆林店 - Refers to a place or inn in Luoyang, indicating a specific location.
  • 中橋 - Refers to a bridge in Luoyang, indicating a specific location.
  • 天津 - Refers to a place or area in Luoyang, indicating a specific location.
  • 上苑 - Refers to a royal garden, indicating a place of beauty and leisure.
  • 紫禁 - Refers to the imperial palace, indicating a place of power and authority.

Proper Nouns

  • 王義方 - The name of the speaker, a former official and teacher.
  • 郭無為 - The name of the esoteric teacher who practiced magical arts.
  • 張孟陽 - The name of the poet who wrote the poem '恭文遥相望原陵郁膴膴'.
  • 原陵 - The tomb of Emperor Guangwu of Han.
  • 北邙 - The northern hills near Luoyang, known for their cemeteries and tombs.
  • 徽安門 - A gate in the city of Luoyang.
  • 榆林店 - A place or inn in Luoyang.
  • 中橋 - A bridge in Luoyang.
  • 天津 - A place or area in Luoyang.
  • 上苑 - A royal garden.
  • 紫禁 - The imperial palace.

[5259]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 老去何譲之遽 - Ambiguous phrase; '老去' means 'in old age,' and '何譲之遽' means 'why yield so hastily.' Expresses a sense of urgency or reluctance to yield to age.
  • 倏然跃入丘中 - '倏然' means 'suddenly,' and '跃入丘中' means 'jump into the hill.' Describes a sudden action.
  • 譲之从焉 - '譲之' means 'the old man,' and '从焉' means 'followed him.' Indicates that the speaker followed the old man.
  • 初入丘曛黑不辨其逐翁已复本形矣 - '初入丘' means 'at first entering the hill,' '曛黑' means 'darkness,' '不辨其逐' means 'cannot distinguish the pursuit,' and '翁已复本形矣' means 'the old man has returned to his original form.' Describes a situation of confusion and transformation.
  • 遂见一狐跳出尾有火防如流星 - '遂见' means 'then saw,' '一狐' means 'a fox,' '跳出' means 'jumped out,' '尾有火防' means 'with a tail like a fire,' and '如流星' means 'like a meteor.' Describes a vivid image of a fox with a fiery tail.
  • 譲之却出堂之外门 - '譲之' means 'the old man,' '却出' means 'stepped out,' '堂' means 'the dark hall,' and '之外门' means 'outside the door.' Indicates that the old man stepped out of the dark hall.
  • 东有一筵已空 - '东' means 'east,' '一筵' means 'a table,' and '已空' means 'already empty.' Describes an empty table in the east.
  • 几案上有硃盏笔砚之 - '几案上' means 'on the table,' '硃盏' means 'red cup,' '笔砚' means 'brush and inkstone,' and '之' means 'such things.' Describes items on the table.
  • 一帖文书尽惨灰色文字则不可晓解略记可辨者 - '一帖文书' means 'a sheet of writing,' '尽' means 'completely worn out,' '惨灰色' means 'dismal gray,' '文字则不可晓解' means 'the words are incomprehensible,' and '略记可辨者' means 'only a few can be recognized.' Describes a worn-out and incomprehensible document.
  • 其一云 - '其一云' means 'the first one says.'
  • 正色鸿焘神思化代穹施后承光设呕沦吐萌垠倪散截迷阳郤曲防【音朦】零【音乙林反】霾曀【入声】雀燬龟冰健驰御屈拿尾研动袾袾防用秘功以岭以穴柂薪伐药莾万茁呕律则祥佛伦惟萨牡虚无有颐咽蘂屑肇素未来晦明兴灭 - Highly cryptic and archaic passage, likely containing esoteric or symbolic language. The words are difficult to translate directly and may require interpretation based on context or historical references.
  • 其二辞曰 - '其二辞曰' means 'the second one says.'
  • 五行七曜成此闰余上帝降灵岁旦涒徐蛇蜕其皮吾亦神攎九九六六束身天除何 - Cryptic passage, likely containing symbolic or esoteric language. The words are difficult to translate directly and may require interpretation based on context or historical references.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 老去 - Refers to old age, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
  • 翁 - Refers to an old man, often used in classical texts to denote wisdom or authority.
  • 狐 - The fox is a common symbol in Chinese folklore, often associated with cunning or supernatural powers.
  • 堂 - Refers to a dark or mysterious hall, often used in classical texts to denote a place of mystery or esoteric knowledge.
  • 硃盏笔砚 - Refers to traditional writing implements, symbolizing scholarly or literary activity.
  • 五行七曜 - Refers to the five elements (metal, wood, water, fire, earth) and the seven luminaries (Sun, Moon, and the five visible planets), common concepts in Chinese cosmology and astrology.
  • 上帝 - Refers to the supreme deity or the heavens, a common concept in Chinese religious and philosophical texts.
  • 岁旦 - Refers to the beginning of the year, often associated with renewal or the passage of time.
  • 涒徐蛇蜕其皮 - Refers to the shedding of a snake's skin, a metaphor for transformation or renewal.
  • 九九六六 - Refers to numbers, often used in Chinese numerology or symbolic contexts.
  • 束身天除 - Refers to binding oneself to the heavens or the divine, a concept often found in religious or philosophical texts.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long sentence describing the old man and the speaker's actions requires careful parsing to understand the sequence of events.
  • The sentence describing the confusion and transformation requires careful parsing to understand the sequence of events.
  • The sentence describing the vivid image of a fox with a fiery tail requires careful parsing to understand the sequence of events.
  • The sentence describing the old man stepping out of the dark hall and finding an empty table in the east requires careful parsing to understand the sequence of events.
  • The sentence describing the items on the table and a worn-out and incomprehensible document requires careful parsing to understand the sequence of events.
  • The cryptic and archaic passage requires careful parsing to understand the sequence of events.
  • The cryptic passage requires careful parsing to understand the sequence of events.

[5260]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 以充喉吐纳太虚 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to fill the throat and breathe in the vast emptiness,' possibly referring to a spiritual or mystical state.
  • 蔽踝霞袂云袽 - Poetic phrase describing something that covers the ankles like a colorful sleeve or cloud-like garment, likely referring to a person's attire or appearance.
  • 哀尔浮生 - Phrase expressing sorrow or pity for the fleeting nature of life, often used to convey a sense of transience.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '成坐令门公远坐坛' is ambiguous and could be interpreted in multiple ways. It could mean 'Cheng sat and ordered Gong Yuan to sit on the altar' or 'Cheng sat, and Gong Yuan sat on the altar.'
  • The complex phrase '彼击余殪尔宜大临吾当以神法缚之' could be broken down into several parts: 'he struck me to death; you should mourn greatly, and I will bind him with divine methods.'
  • The phrase '及其击也公远仆地' could be interpreted as 'when he struck, Gong Yuan fell to the ground' or 'Gong Yuan fell to the ground when he struck.'
  • The phrase '成大战恐自言力竭变成老狐' could be interpreted as 'Cheng fought fiercely, afraid and said his strength was exhausted, transforming into an old fox.'

  • 及至车骑辉赫傧从风流三十余人 - Describes the wedding procession: 'When they arrived, the carriages and horses were splendid, and there were more than thirty attendants.'
  • 至韦氏送杂防五十匹红罗五十匹他物称是 - Describes the gifts: 'They brought fifty pieces of silk, fifty pieces of red silk, and other things of similar value.'

  • 答云八叔房小妺今颇成人叔父令事高门 - Describes the fox's response: 'Cui replied, 'The younger sister of my uncle has grown up, and my uncle wants to marry her into a noble family.''
  • 其所以病者小妺入室故也 - Describes the cause of the illness: 'The reason for the illness is that the younger sister has entered the inner chambers.'
  • 母极骂云死野狐魅你公然魅我一女不足更恼我儿吾夫妇暮年唯仰此子与汝野狐为婚绝吾继嗣耶 - Describes the mother's reaction: 'The mother scolded fiercely, 'You're a dead fox spirit! You openly bewitched my daughter, and now you're causing my son to fall ill. My husband and I are old, and we only have this son. Marrying a fox spirit like you will cut off our lineage!'
  • 崔无言但欢笑 - Describes the fox's response: 'Cui remained silent but laughed.'
  • 父母日夕拜请绐云尔若能愈儿疾女寔不敢复论 - Describes the parents' plea: 'The parents begged day and night, saying, 'If you can cure our son's illness, we won't say anything about the daughter.''
  • 久之乃云疾愈易得但恐心耳 - Describes the fox's response: 'After a long time, Cui said, 'It's easy to cure the illness, but I'm afraid of your heart.'
  • 母频为设盟誓异日崔乃于怀出一文字令母效书及取 - Describes the fox's demand: 'The mother repeatedly made vows. One day, Cui took out a piece of paper from his sleeve and asked the mother to copy it.'

  • 天曹知此事杖我几死 - '天曹' refers to the heavenly bureaucracy, and '杖我几死' means 'beat me almost to death,' indicating that the fox was punished by the heavens.

  • 穷老魅何不速行敢此逗遛耶 - '穷老魅' refers to the fox as an old, poor spirit, and '何不速行敢此逗遛耶' means 'why not hurry and leave, why linger here?' indicating a command to the fox.
  • 独不念我钱物恩耶 - '独不念' means 'do not even remember,' and '我钱物恩' means 'my financial and material kindness,' indicating that the fox does not remember the help given.
  • 坐偷用天府中钱今无可还受此荼毒 - '坐偷用' means 'because of stealing and using,' '天府中钱' means 'money from the heavenly treasury,' and '受此荼毒' means 'suffer this torment,' indicating that the fox is being punished for stealing.

  • 出广异记 - '出广异记' means 'from the *Guangyi Ji*,' indicating the source of the story.
  • 唐林景者京兆人侨居鴈门以骑射畋猎为已任郡守悦其能因募为衙门将 - '唐林景者' refers to a person named Lin Jingxuan from the Tang dynasty, '京兆人' means 'from Jingzhao (a region),' '侨居鴈门' means 'resided in Yanmen,' '以骑射畋猎为已任' means 'took riding, shooting, and hunting as his duties,' '郡守悦其能' means 'the county magistrate was pleased with his abilities,' and '因募为衙门将' means 'thus recruited him as a military leader.'
  • 尝与其徒十数辈驰健马执矢兵杖臂隼牵犬俱骋于田野间得麋鹿狐兎甚多 - '尝与其徒十数辈' means 'once with his dozen or so followers,' '驰健马' means 'rode strong horses,' '执矢兵杖' means 'carried bows, arrows, and weapons,' '臂隼' means 'with falcons on their arms,' '牵犬' means 'with dogs on leashes,' '俱骋于田野间' means 'all galloped in the fields,' and '得麋鹿狐兎甚多' means 'caught many deer, foxes, and rabbits.'
  • 由是郡守纵其所往不使亲吏事 - '由是' means 'therefore,' '郡守纵其所往' means 'the county magistrate allowed him to go wherever he wanted,' and '不使亲吏事' means 'did not make him handle official matters.'

  • 霍邑古吕州也 - Literal translation: 'Huo County is the ancient Lu Prefecture.'
  • 城池甚固 - Literal translation: 'The city walls are very strong.'
  • 县令宅东北有城面各百歩 - Literal translation: 'There is a city gate to the northeast of the magistrate's residence, each side measuring one hundred steps.'
  • 其高三丈厚七八尺 - Literal translation: 'It is three zhang high and seven or eight chi thick.'
  • 名曰囚周厉王城 - Literal translation: 'It is named the Prison of King Li of Zhou.'
  • 则左传所称万人不流王于城即霍邑也 - Literal translation: 'This is the city mentioned in the *Zuo Zhuan* where ten thousand people did not allow King Li to be exiled to the city of Shu, which is Huo County.'
  • 王崩因葬城之北 - Literal translation: 'After the king's death, he was buried to the north of the city.'
  • 城旣久逺则有魅狐居之 - Literal translation: 'Since the city has been far from recent times, there are now foxes and ghosts inhabiting it.'
  • 或官吏家或百姓子女姿色者夜中狐防其髪有如刀截所遇无知往往而有 - Literal translation: 'Sometimes the foxes would attack the hair of officials' families or beautiful young women at night, as if cutting with a knife. Those who encountered this often lost their hair.'
  • 唐时邑人靳守贞者素善符呪为县送徒至赵城还归至金狗鼻傍汾河山名去县五里见汾河西岸水滨有女红裳浣衣水次守贞目之女子忽尔乘空过河遂缘岭蹑虚至守贞所手攀其笠足其将取其髪焉 - Literal translation: 'During the Tang Dynasty, a resident of the county named Jin Shouzhen, who was skilled in charms and spells, was sent to Zhao City to escort prisoners. On his return, he reached the vicinity of Jindogou, near the Fen River, five li from the county. He saw a girl in red clothes washing clothes by the water. Shouzhen watched her, and the girl suddenly flew over the river, climbed a hill, and approached Shouzhen, grabbing his hat and trying to cut his hair.'
  • 守贞送徒手犹持斧因击女子坠从而斫之女子死则为雌狐守贞以狐至县具列其由县令不之信守贞归遂每夜有老父及媪绕其居哭从索其女守贞不惧月余老父及媪骂而去曰无状杀我女吾犹有三女终当困汝于是遂絶而截髪亦亡 - Literal translation: 'Shouzhen, still holding an axe, struck the girl, causing her to fall and die. She turned into a female fox. Shouzhen brought the fox to the county and reported the incident, but the magistrate did not believe him. Shouzhen returned home, and every night, an old man and an old woman would cry around his house, seeking their daughter. Shouzhen was not afraid. After a month, the old man and woman cursed him and left, saying, 'You wickedly killed our daughter. We still have three daughters, and we will eventually trouble you.' From then on, Shouzhen's hair was cut off, and he disappeared.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '或官吏家或百姓子女姿色者夜中狐防其髪有如刀截所遇无知往往而有' is somewhat elliptical and could be clearer in English. It describes a supernatural event where foxes attack the hair of certain people.
  • The long sentence '唐时邑人靳守贞者素善符呪为县送徒至赵城还归至金狗鼻傍汾河山名去县五里见汾河西岸水滨有女红裳浣衣水次守贞目之女子忽尔乘空过河遂缘岭蹑虚至守贞所手攀其笠足其将取其髪焉' describes a detailed encounter with a supernatural being. It could be broken down into simpler clauses in English.
  • The long sentence '守贞送徒手犹持斧因击女子坠从而斫之女子死则为雌狐守贞以狐至县具列其由县令不之信守贞归遂每夜有老父及媪绕其居哭从索其女守贞不惧月余老父及媪骂而去曰无状杀我女吾犹有三女终当困汝于是遂絶而截髪亦亡' describes the aftermath of the encounter and the supernatural retribution. It could be broken down into simpler clauses in English.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • Zhang Li, the magistrate of Shifeng during the Tang dynasty, suffered from a disease caused by demons, which would occasionally flare up. His family could not control it. He would stretch his right arm and recite a spell: 'Fox spirit, be strong.' His son secretly held an iron pestle, waiting for Zhang Li's disease to flare up and then striking him from behind. A large female fox fell and was burned at a major intersection. From then on, Zhang Li recovered.
  • 太平广记巻四百五十 - Chapter title in the *Taiping Guangji*, a collection of stories and anecdotes from Chinese history, compiled during the Song dynasty.

  • 狐五 - Subcategory in the *Taiping Guangji* focusing on fox spirits.

  • The phrase '回顾简栖曰谢以册子相还' is elliptical and requires context to understand that Jianqi looked back at Jianqi, he said, 'Thank you for returning the booklet to me.'

  • 少顷有秉炬自内至者振管辟扉引保母出知古前拜仍述厥由 - Means 'a short while later, someone carrying a torch came from inside. He shook the tube (perhaps a signaling device) and opened the door, leading the nursemaid out. The young man bowed before her and recounted his reason.'
  • 母曰夫人传语主与小子皆不在家于礼无延客之道然僻居与山薮接畛豺狼所嘷若固相拒是见溺而不援也请舎外防翌日可去 - Means 'The nursemaid said, 'The lady conveyed that the master and the young master are not at home. According to propriety, there is no way to entertain guests. However, this remote place borders the mountains and forests where wolves howl. If we were to reject you outright, it would be like seeing someone drown and not helping. Please stay outside for the night; you can leave tomorrow.'
  • 知古辞谢从保母而入过重门侧防所栾栌宏敞帷幕鲜华张银灯设绮席命知古坐焉酒三行复陈方丈之馔豹胎鲂腴穷水陆之羙者保母亦时来相勉食毕保母复问知古世嗣官族及内外姻党知古具言之乃曰秀才轩裳令胄金玉竒标既富春秋又洁操履斯实淑媛之贤夫也小君以钟爱稚女将及笄年常托媒妁为求佳对乆矣今夕何夕获遘良人潘杨之睦可遵鳯凰之兆斯 - Means 'the young man bowed and followed the nursemaid inside. Passed through the heavy doors and the side gate, where the beams and rafters were grand and the curtains were fresh and beautiful. Silver lamps were lit, and a brocade mat was set up, and the young man was invited to sit. After three rounds of wine, a lavish feast was served, including delicacies like leopard fetus, carp, and the finest of both land and sea. The nursemaid occasionally came to encourage him. After the meal, the nursemaid asked the young man about his family, lineage, and in-laws. The young man answered in detail, saying, 'I am a scholar, born into a noble family, with a pure and upright character; I am young but of good conduct. I am indeed a worthy husband for a virtuous lady. The lady, who cherishes her young daughter, has long sought a good match for her as she approaches the age of marriage. Tonight, what a night it is! We have found a good match, a union like the harmony of the Pan and Yang families, which can follow the auspicious omen of the phoenix.'

  • 主人遗绢数十匹以谢之 - 'The master gave him dozens of bolts of silk as a gift of thanks'; '遗绢' refers to giving silk as a gift, a common form of gratitude in ancient China.
  • 须得傔力 - 'He needed a servant’s strength'; '傔力' refers to a servant or attendant.

  • 谨纳之 - 'Jin accepted them'; a formal way of expressing acceptance.

  • 将及关归宝忽大骂曰 - 'As they were about to reach the pass, Gui Bao suddenly shouted angrily, saying…'; '将及关' means 'as they were about to reach the pass.'
  • 以我为奴如役汝父 - 'You treat me as a slave, just as you would your father'; this is an accusation of mistreatment.
  • 因絶走谨骇怒逐之其行如风倐忽不见 - 'He then ran away, and Jin, in great anger, chased him, but he vanished like the wind, suddenly disappearing'; '絶走' means to run away suddenly or decisively.
  • 既而德儿亦不见所赍之物皆失之矣 - 'Then De Er also disappeared, and all the items he had carried were lost'; '赍' means to carry or bear, often with a respectful or official connotation.
  • 时秦陇用兵关禁严 - 'At that time, there was war in Qin and Long, and the border checkpoints were strictly guarded'; '秦陇' refers to regions in northwest China, and '用兵' means war or military action.
  • 客行无验皆见刑戮 - 'Travelers without proper credentials were all subjected to punishment or execution'; '验' here refers to credentials or verification.
  • 既不敢东度复还主人具以告之 - 'He dared not go east, so he returned to the master and fully reported the situation'; '具以告之' means to fully or completely report.
  • 主人怒曰寜有是事是无厌复将挠我耳 - 'The master angrily said, 'Could there be such a thing? This is just a way to be insatiable and annoy me again'; '寜有是事' is a rhetorical question meaning 'Could there be such a thing?'
  • 因憇大树下仰见二儿曰吾德儿归宝也汝之为奴苦否 - 'He then rested under a big tree and saw the two boys, saying, 'This is my De Er and Gui Bao. Did your life as a servant cause you suffering?'; '憇' means to rest or take a break.
  • 此符法我之书也失之已久今喜再获吾岂无情于汝乎 - 'This talisman method is my writing. I lost it a long time ago, but now I am happy to have recovered it. Could I be without feeling for you?'; '符法' refers to the method or technique of using talismans.
  • 因掷行李还之曰速归乡人待尔书符也即大笑而去 - 'He then threw the luggage back to them, saying, 'Go quickly back to your village; your people are waiting for you to write talismans.' Then he laughed and disappeared'; '掷' means to throw, and '书符' is to write a talisman.

  • 蛇入堂中心有一孔大如盆口蛇入并尽 - Means 'the snakes entered the hall, and in the center, there was a hole as large as a basin's mouth; the snakes entered and were all gone.'
  • 令煎汤一百斛灌之 - Means 'they ordered to boil one hundred shi of water and pour it over it.'
  • 经宿以锹掘之深数尺得古铜钱二十万贯 - Means 'after a night, they dug with a shovel several feet deep and found twenty million strings of ancient copper coins.'
  • 因陈破铸新钱遂巨富 - Means 'they then melted down the old coins to cast new ones and became very wealthy.'
  • 蛇乃是古铜之精 - Means 'the snakes were actually the essence of ancient copper.'
  • 搜神记蛇千年则断复续 - From the *Search for Spirits*, it states that a snake after a thousand years can break and then reconnect.
  • 淮南子云神蛇自断其身而自相续 - From the *Huainanzi*, it is said that a divine snake can sever its body and then reconnect itself.
  • 隋炀帝遣人于岭南邉海穷山求此蛇数四而至洛下 - Means 'Emperor Yang of Sui sent people to the remote mountains near the southern sea in Lingnan to seek this snake, which came to Luoyang several times.'
  • 所得之者长可三尺而色黄黒其头锦文全似金色不能毒人觧食肉 - Means 'what was obtained was about three feet long, with a yellowish-black color, a head adorned with patterns resembling gold, and it could not poison people, and its meat could be eaten.'
  • 若欲令自断其身者则先触之令怒使不任其愤毒则自断为三四其断之处如刀截焉 - Means 'if one wants to make it sever its own body, first provoke it to anger, and when it cannot bear its rage, it will sever itself into two or three pieces, the cuts being as sharp as if made by a knife.'
  • 见其皮骨文理亦有血焉良乆怒定则三四断稍稍自相就而连续体复如故亦似不相断 - Means 'when you see its skin and bones, there is also blood. After a long time, when its anger subsides, the two or three pieces will gradually come together and reconnect, returning to its original state, as if it had not been severed at all.'
  • 隋著作郎邓隆云此灵蛇一自断不必千嵗也 - Means 'the Sui Dynasty's historiographer Deng Long said that this divine snake can sever itself once, but it is not necessarily after a thousand years.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 隋绛州夏县树提家 - Refers to a family named Shu Ti in Xia County, Jiang Prefecture, during the Sui Dynasty.
  • 隋炀帝 - Refers to Emperor Yang of Sui, the second emperor of the Sui Dynasty.
  • 岭南邉海穷山 - Refers to the remote mountains near the southern sea in Lingnan, a region in southern China.
  • 洛下 - Refers to Luoyang, the capital of the Sui Dynasty.
  • 搜神记 - *Search for Spirits*, an ancient Chinese collection of strange stories and supernatural events.
  • 淮南子 - *Huainanzi*, a philosophical text from the Han Dynasty that includes various stories and theories.
  • 隋著作郎邓隆 - Refers to Deng Long, a historian and scholar during the Sui Dynasty.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 觧符镇取桃枝四枚书符绕宅四面钉之 - The sequence of actions must be read as a single process: taking peach branches, writing charms, and nailing them around the house.
  • 蛇渐退符亦移就之 - The phrase '符亦移就之' indicates that the charms moved with the snakes as they retreated.
  • 蛇入堂中心有一孔大如盆口蛇入并尽 - The phrase '蛇入并尽' means that the snakes entered the hole and were all gone.
  • 令煎汤一百斛灌之 - The phrase '灌之' means 'pour it over it,' referring to pouring the boiling water over the area where the snakes were.
  • 经宿以锹掘之深数尺得古铜钱二十万贯 - The phrase '经宿' means 'after a night,' indicating the time taken for the digging.
  • 因陈破铸新钱遂巨富 - The phrase '因陈破铸新钱' means 'they then melted down the old coins to cast new ones,' leading to their wealth.
  • 蛇乃是古铜之精 - The phrase '古铜之精' means 'the essence of ancient copper,' suggesting the supernatural nature of the snakes.
  • 搜神记蛇千年则断复续 - The phrase '断复续' means 'break and then reconnect,' describing a unique property of the snake.
  • 淮南子云神蛇自断其身而自相续 - The phrase '自断其身而自相续' means 'sever its body and then reconnect itself,' further describing the snake's supernatural ability.
  • 隋炀帝遣人于岭南邉海穷山求此蛇数四而至洛下 - The phrase '数四而至洛下' means 'which came to Luoyang several times,' indicating the repeated efforts to obtain the snake.
  • 所得之者长可三尺而色黄黒其头锦文全似金色不能毒人觧食肉 - The phrase '长可三尺而色黄黒' describes the physical appearance of the snake, and '不能毒人觧食肉' means it is not poisonous and its meat can be eaten.
  • 若欲令自断其身者则先触之令怒使不任其愤毒则自断为三四其断之处如刀截焉 - The phrase '若欲令自断其身者' means 'if one wants to make it sever its own body,' and '其断之处如刀截焉' means 'the cuts being as sharp as if made by a knife.'
  • 见其皮骨文理亦有血焉良乆怒定则三四断稍稍自相就而连续体复如故亦似不相断 - The phrase '良乆怒定则三四断' means 'after a long time, when its anger subsides, the two or three pieces will gradually come together,' and '连续体复如故' means 'returning to its original state.'
  • 隋著作郎邓隆云此灵蛇一自断不必千嵗也 - The phrase '一自断' means 'can sever itself once,' and '不必千嵗也' means 'but it is not necessarily after a thousand years.'

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 孤岛焉舟人因是乗风举帆遂得免难 - On an isolated island, the boatman, therefore, sailed with the wind and avoided disaster.
  • 后数日复至一山遥见烟火谓是人境落帆登陵与二人同行门户甚大遂前欵关 - After several days, he reached another mountain, saw smoke and fire from afar, thought it was a human settlement, dropped anchor and climbed the hill, walked with two people, saw a very large gate, and then approached the gate.
  • 高宗光宅中李崇贞仕益州长史防前柑子树有一子如鸡子晚熟微有小孔如针羣官咸异之方欲将进乆而乃罢因剖之得一赤斑蛇长尺余崇贞后竟以罪死 - During the reign of Emperor Gaozong in Guangzhi, Li Chongzhen served as the Chief Secretary of Yizhou. There was a fruit on a citron tree like a chicken egg, which ripened late with a small hole like a needle. All officials found it strange, and they wanted to present it but eventually gave up. When they cut it open, they found a red-spotted snake about a foot long. Li Chongzhen was later executed for a crime.
  • 又连州见一柑树四月中有子如拳大剖之有两头蛇 - In Lianzhou, there was a citron tree. In April, there was a fruit as big as a fist. When cut open, there was a two-headed snake.
  • 开元四年六月郴州马岭山侧有白蛇长六七尺黒蛇长丈余须臾二蛇鬬白者吞黒蛇到麄处口两嗌皆裂血流滂沛黒蛇头入囓白蛇肋上作孔头出二尺余俄而两蛇并死后十余日大雨山水暴涨漂殁五百余家失三百余人 - In June of the fourth year of Kaiyuan, on the side of Malingshan in Chenzhou, there was a white snake six or seven feet long and a black snake over ten feet long. Soon the two snakes fought. The white one swallowed the black one, but at a thick part, both mouths split open, and blood flowed profusely. The black snake's head went into the white snake's side, making a hole and coming out two feet. Soon both snakes died. After more than ten days, heavy rain caused the water to rise, drowning over 500 families and leaving 300 people missing.

  • 开元 - The Kaiyuan era, a period of prosperity and cultural flourishing in the Tang Dynasty.
  • 郴州 - Chenzhou, a city in Hunan Province.
  • 马岭山 - Malingshan, a mountain in Chenzhou.

Syntactic Ambiguities

Then they sailed with the wind for five or six days, saw an island from afar, and anchored the boat. They asked someone and learned it was the boundary of Qingyuan County, belonging to Nanhai.'

When they cut it open, they found a red-spotted snake about a foot long. Li Chongzhen was later executed for a crime.'
  • This phrase can be read as 'In Lianzhou, there was a citron tree. In April, there was a fruit as big as a fist. When cut open, there was a two-headed snake.'

Soon the two snakes fought. The white one swallowed the black one, but at a thick part, both mouths split open, and blood flowed profusely. The black snake's head went into the white snake's side, making a hole and coming out two feet. Soon both snakes died.   • 贤者初修道时蛇大一围 - When the sage first practiced Daoism, the snake was as thick as a man's arm.

[5376]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 临淮郡有馆亭滨泗水上 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'there was a pavilion by the side of the Si River in Linhuai Prefecture.' Preserves spatial relationship.
  • 周数十拱 - Measure of circumference, meaning 'surrounding several dozen gong' (unit of measurement). Exact meaning of gong is unclear but likely refers to diameter or circumference.
  • 突然劲防 - Metaphorical or poetic phrase describing the tree as 'suddenly strong and firm,' or 'suddenly rising in strength and defense.'
  • 人望见亭有二光对而上下赫然 - 'People saw two lights facing each other, rising and falling brightly.' Metaphorical or supernatural description of lights above the pavilion.
  • 风雷既息其光亦闭 - 'Once the wind and thunder ceased, the light also closed.' Poetic way of saying the lights disappeared after the storm passed.
  • 开元中有韦子春以勇力闻 - 'During the Kaiyuan era, there was a man named Wei Zichun who was known for his courage and strength.' Kaiyuan is a reign title of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang and should be preserved.
  • 防子春客于临淮 - 'Someone told Wei Zichun about the incident while he was a guest in Linhuai.' Term '防' is likely a typo for '访' (to visit or inform), and should be rendered as 'visited' or 'told.'
  • 吾能伺之 - 'I can watch for it.' '伺' means to observe or wait for something, often with a sense of anticipation or vigilance.
  • 遂有大风雷震于地 - 'Then there was a great wind and thunder that shook the earth.' Dramatic description of a storm event.
  • 亭屋摇撼 - 'The pavilion's roof shook.' Direct description of the storm's effect on the structure.
  • 騞然有声 - 'A huo sound was made.' '騞' is a onomatopoeic character, possibly representing the sound of a blade or a strike.
  • 腥若鲍肆 - 'Smelled like a fish market.' '鲍肆' refers to a shop selling baowu (a type of dried fish or shellfish), used metaphorically for a foul or fishy odor.
  • 里人相与来观 - 'The people of the village came to see.' '里人' refers to local residents or villagers.
  • 谓子春且死矣 - 'They thought Wei Zichun would die.' '且' is used here to mean 'about to' or 'would.'
  • 自是其亭无风雷患 - 'From then on, the pavilion had no more wind and thunder troubles.' Conclusion drawn from the event indicating Wei Zichun's intervention had a supernatural effect.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 临淮郡 - Historical prefecture in ancient China, likely referring to the area of modern-day Jiangsu or Anhui provinces.
  • 泗水 - The Si River, a major river in eastern China, historically associated with the state of Lu and the Confucian tradition.
  • 开元 - The Kaiyuan era, a period of prosperity and cultural flourishing during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of the Tang Dynasty (713–741 CE). Should be preserved as is.
  • 韦子春 - A historical or legendary figure, likely a folk hero or warrior from the Tang period. His name should be translated as 'Wei Zichun.'
  • 宣室志 - The Xuan Shi Zhi (The Records of the Xuan Room), a collection of supernatural tales from the Tang Dynasty, attributed to Zhang Zhuo. This is a canonical source and should be cited as is.
  • 蛇 - The Chinese word for 'snake,' often used in classical Chinese texts to symbolize danger, transformation, or supernatural forces.
  • 鲍肆 - A shop selling baowu, a type of preserved fish or shellfish. The term is used metaphorically here to describe a foul or fishy smell, likely from the dead snake.

Proper Nouns

  • 临淮郡 - Linhuai Prefecture (modern-day Jiangsu or Anhui)
  • 泗水 - Si River
  • 开元 - Kaiyuan era (Tang Dynasty)
  • 韦子春 - Wei Zichun
  • 宣室志 - Xuan Shi Zhi (The Records of the Xuan Room)

The term '多心' (multitude of heart) is somewhat unusual and may be a variant or mistranscription of '心' (heart).

  • 或遗人以充口腹 - Means 'some people give them to others to satisfy their appetite.'
  • 或杀之以为脯腊 - Means 'some people kill them to make them into dried meat.'
  • 人又养其雏为媒旁施网罟捕野孔雀 - Means 'people also raise their chicks as decoys and set nets to catch wild peacocks.'
  • 伺其飞下则牵网横掩之 - Means 'waiting for them to fly down and then pull the net across to trap them.'
  • 采其金翠毛装为扇拂 - Means 'they collect their golden and green feathers to decorate fans.'
  • 或全株生截其尾以为方物云 - Means 'or they cut off their tails while they are still alive and use them as tribute.'
  • 生取则金翠之色不减耳 - Means 'if taken alive, the golden and green colors do not diminish.'
  • 罗州 - Refers to Luo Zhou, an ancient Chinese prefecture in what is now southern China.
  • 罗州山中多孔雀羣飞者数十为偶 - Means 'in the mountains of Luo Zhou, there are many peacocks that fly in groups of tens as pairs.'
  • 雌者尾短无金翠 - Means 'the females have short tails without golden and green feathers.'
  • 雄者生三年有小尾五年成大尾始春而生三四月后复凋 - Means 'the males have small tails at three years old and full tails at five years old, which start to grow in spring and fall off in April or May.'
  • 与花蕚相荣衰 - Means 'they grow and fade with the flowers.'
  • 自喜其尾而甚妬 - Means 'they are very proud of their tails but very jealous.'
  • 凡欲山栖必先择有置尾之地然后止焉 - Means 'whenever they want to roost in the mountains, they first choose a place to display their tails and then settle there.'
  • 南人生捕者甚雨往擒之尾霑而重不能髙翔 - Means 'southern people who catch them wait for heavy rain and then catch them; the wet tails make them too heavy to fly high.'
  • 人虽至且爱其尾恐人所伤不复骞翔也 - Means 'even though people come near, they still love their tails and fear being hurt, so they do not fly away.'
  • 虽驯养颇久见羙妇人好衣裳与童子丝服者必逐而啄之 - Means 'even after being tamed for a long time, they will chase and peck at beautiful women and children wearing silk clothes.'
  • 芳时媚景闻管笙歌必舒张翅尾盻睇而舞若有意焉 - Means 'during beautiful times and charming scenes, when they hear music, they spread their wings and tails and dance as if they have intent.'
  • 山谷夷民烹而食之味如鹅解百毒 - Means 'the hill and valley natives cook and eat them, and the taste is like that of a goose; they can neutralize all poisons.'
  • 人食其肉饮药不能愈病 - Means 'people who eat their meat and take medicine cannot be cured of their illnesses.'

  • 如竟午不获即空中逐而涎惹之无不中焉 - Explains what the bird does if it fails to catch prey by noon: it flies into the air and uses its saliva to catch prey, always succeeding.
  • 人若捕得脯治渴其涎每布后半日即干自落落即布之 - Describes the practical use of the bird and its saliva: if humans capture the bird, its meat can be used to quench thirst, and the saliva will dry and fall off after half a day, making it usable again.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 韩朋鸟者乃凫鹥之 - 韩朋鸟属于凫鹥之类，指韩朋鸟是一种水鸟。
  • 此鸟为飞泛溪浦水禽中鸂鶒鸳鸯防防岭北皆有之 - 这种鸟在溪流和水边飞翔，像鸂鶒、鸳鸯一样，岭北地区都有。
  • 唯韩朋鸟未之见也 - 只有韩朋鸟未曾见过，强调韩朋鸟的稀有性。
  • 案干寳搜神记云 - 根据干宝的《搜神记》记载。
  • 大夫韩朋【一云凭】其妻美宋康王夺之朋怨王囚之朋遂自杀 - 大夫韩朋的妻子美丽，被宋康王夺走，韩朋怨恨宋康王，被囚禁后自杀。
  • 妻乃阴腐其衣 - 妻子偷偷将衣服腐烂，暗示她对丈夫的忠诚和哀悼。
  • 王与之登台自投台下 - 宋康王带她登上高台，她跳下台。
  • 左右提衣衣不胜手遗书于带曰愿以尸还韩氏而合 - 左右的人提着她的衣服，衣服承受不住，她在腰带上留下信件说希望尸体能归还给韩家，合葬在一起。
  • 王怒令埋之以塜相望 - 宋康王愤怒，命令将他们分别埋葬，坟墓相对。
  • 经宿忽见有梓木生二塜之上根交于下枝连其上 - 一夜之后忽然看见梓木在两个坟墓上生长，根部交错，枝条相连。
  • 又有鸟如鸳鸯恒栖其树朝暮悲鸣 - 还有像鸳鸯一样的鸟常栖息在树上，早晚悲鸣。
  • 南人谓此禽即韩朋夫妇之精魂故以韩氏名之 - 南方人认为这种鸟就是韩朋夫妇的精魂，因此以韩氏命名。
  • 带箭鸟鸣如野鹊翅羽黄緑间错尾生两枝长二尺余直而不枭 - 带箭鸟的叫声像野鹊，翅膀和羽毛黄绿相间，尾巴生有两枝，长二尺多，直而不弯。
  • 唯尾稍有毛宛如箭羽因目之为带箭鸟 - 尾巴稍有羽毛，像箭羽一样，因此称为带箭鸟。
  • 汉元封五年勒毕国贡细鸟以方尺玉笼盛数百头大如蝇其状如鹦鹉闻声数里如黄鹄之音 - 汉元封五年，勒毕国进贡细鸟，用一尺见方的玉笼盛放，有数百只，大小如蝇，形状像鹦鹉，声音传数里，像黄鹄的声音。
  • 国人常以此鸟候时亦名曰候虫 - 国人常用这种鸟来预测季节，也称为候虫。
  • 上得之放于宫内旬日之间不知所止惜甚求不复得 - 皇帝得到后放在宫内，十天之内不知去向，非常可惜，再也找不到了。
  • 明年此鸟复来集于帷幄之上或入衣袖因更名曰蝉鸟 - 第二年这种鸟又来了，聚集在帷幄之上，有时进入衣袖，因此改名为蝉鸟。
  • 宫人婕妤等皆恱之但有此鸟集于衣上者辄爱幸 - 宫人和婕妤等都喜欢它，只要有这种鸟聚集在衣上的人就会受到宠爱。
  • 武帝末稍稍自死人尤爱其皮服其皮者多为男子媚也 - 武帝末年，这种鸟渐渐自己死去，人们尤其喜爱它的皮，穿这种皮的人多能吸引男子。
